input:
You are given a Malay sentence in which one discourse particle has been replaced with [___].
Your task is to predict which particle, either “ke,” “kan,” or “neutral,” belongs in the [___] slot, such that the discourse-context attributes for this sentence are The speaker treats the statement as already true or established — no hedging, no doubt, full confidence.
The particle appears anywhere other than the front — mid-sentence or at the end.
No clear orientation toward listener agreement; a plain statement or ambiguous stance.
No detectable emotional charge, or the emotion is genuinely ambiguous.
Phrased as a question but does not expect a genuine answer; used to make a point or emphasise something..

Particle meanings:
  ke  : signals genuine uncertainty or invites the listener to confirm something the speaker is unsure about.
  kan : signals assumed shared knowledge and seeks light confirmation of something the speaker already believes ("right?").
  neutral : indicates no particle.

Speaker:
  "Menggatal/Tuaran no electric since 8:50pm. Ada [___] notis dari tadi sy check fb sesb tda notis. Tu karen kan cukup2 utk buka 1 lampu ja"



Using the sentence context and the attributes above, which single particle — "ke" or "kan" or "neutral" — best fills [___]?

Return exactly one word from this set and nothing else: ke, kan, neutral
constraint: You are a linguist specialising in colloquial Malay discourse particles. You must output exactly one word — either "ke" or "kan" or "neutral" — and nothing else.

Your task is to predict which particle, either “ke,” “kan,” or “neutral,” belongs in the [___] slot, such that the discourse-context attributes for this sentence are The speaker treats the statement as already true or established — no hedging, no doubt, full confidence.

No clear orientation toward listener agreement; a plain statement or ambiguous stance.
The speaker expresses frustration, annoyance, disappointment, sadness, anger, or bitterness.
The sentence makes an assertion or conveys information — not structured as a question..

Particle meanings:
  ke  : signals genuine uncertainty or invites the listener to confirm something the speaker is unsure about.
  kan : signals assumed shared knowledge and seeks light confirmation of something the speaker already believes ("right?").
  neutral : indicates no particle.

Speaker:
  "Trs yaudahlah [___] dah slesai smua dah plg lah kita.
Trs tinggalah aku dan mood ku ni yg lg bertarung siapakah yg menang"

Return exactly one word from this set and nothing else: ke, kan, neutral
constraint: You are a linguist specialising in colloquial Malay discourse particles. You must output exactly one word — either "ke" or "kan" or "neutral" — and nothing else.

Your task is to predict which particle, either “ke,” “kan,” or “neutral,” belongs in the [___] slot, such that the discourse-context attributes for this sentence are The speaker sounds unsure, is making a guess, estimating, or checking whether something is the case.
The particle appears anywhere other than the front — mid-sentence or at the end.
The speaker is actively checking whether the listener agrees or can confirm something.
The speaker expresses frustration, annoyance, disappointment, sadness, anger, or bitterness.
A genuine question inviting the listener to confirm or deny; the speaker does not already know the answer..

Particle meanings:
  ke  : signals genuine uncertainty or invites the listener to confirm something the speaker is unsure about.
  kan : signals assumed shared knowledge and seeks light confirmation of something the speaker already believes ("right?").
  neutral : indicates no particle.

Punah..."

Your task is to predict which particle, either “ke,” “kan,” or “neutral,” belongs in the [___] slot, such that the discourse-context attributes for this sentence are The speaker sounds unsure, is making a guess, estimating, or checking whether something is the case.
The particle appears anywhere other than the front — mid-sentence or at the end.
The speaker treats the information as shared or obvious — the underlying tone is 'you already know this'.
The speaker expresses frustration, annoyance, disappointment, sadness, anger, or bitterness.
Phrased as a question but does not expect a genuine answer; used to make a point or emphasise something..

Particle meanings:
  ke  : signals genuine uncertainty or invites the listener to confirm something the speaker is unsure about.
  kan : signals assumed shared knowledge and seeks light confirmation of something the speaker already believes ("right?").
  neutral : indicates no particle.

Speaker:
  "RT @QS09_51: Lawatan rasmi ada prior notice [___]? Atau ampa main serbu pastu claim lawatan rasmi?

Mangkaq ubi.

Dah kantoi, pi report polis…"

Return exactly one word from this set and nothing else: ke, kan, neutral
constraint: You are a linguist specialising in colloquial Malay discourse particles. You must output exactly one word — either "ke" or "kan" or "neutral" — and nothing else.

Your task is to predict which particle, either “ke,” “kan,” or “neutral,” belongs in the [___] slot, such that the discourse-context attributes for this sentence are The speaker treats the statement as already true or established — no hedging, no doubt, full confidence.
The particle appears anywhere other than the front — mid-sentence or at the end.
The speaker treats the information as shared or obvious — the underlying tone is 'you already know this'.
The speaker expresses happiness, excitement, enthusiasm, satisfaction, humour, affection, or relief.
Phrased as a question but does not expect a genuine answer; used to make a point or emphasise something..

Particle meanings:
  ke  : signals genuine uncertainty or invites the listener to confirm something the speaker is unsure about.
  kan : signals assumed shared knowledge and seeks light confirmation of something the speaker already believes ("right?").
  neutral : indicates no particle.

#MajalahRemaja #Remaja #RemajaMalaysia

https://t.co/o701lpSLhp"



Using the sentence context and the attributes above, which single particle — "ke" or "kan" or "neutral" — best fills [___]?

Return exactly one word from this set and nothing else: ke, kan, neutral
constraint: You are a linguist specialising in colloquial Malay discourse particles. You must output exactly one word — either "ke" or "kan" or "neutral" — and nothing else.

Your task is to predict which particle, either “ke,” “kan,” or “neutral,” belongs in the [___] slot, such that the discourse-context attributes for this sentence are The speaker treats the statement as already true or established — no hedging, no doubt, full confidence.
The particle appears anywhere other than the front — mid-sentence or at the end.
The speaker treats the information as shared or obvious — the underlying tone is 'you already know this'.
The speaker expresses happiness, excitement, enthusiasm, satisfaction, humour, affection, or relief.
The sentence makes an assertion or conveys information — not structured as a question..

Particle meanings:
  ke  : signals genuine uncertainty or invites the listener to confirm something the speaker is unsure about.
  kan : signals assumed shared knowledge and seeks light confirmation of something the speaker already believes ("right?").
  neutral : indicates no particle.

Speaker:
  "@simplyjoyaa betul betul betul, kita [___] harus baik"

Return exactly one word from this set and nothing else: ke, kan, neutral
constraint: You are a linguist specialising in colloquial Malay discourse particles. You must output exactly one word — either "ke" or "kan" or "neutral" — and nothing else.

Your task is to predict which particle, either “ke,” “kan,” or “neutral,” belongs in the [___] slot, such that the discourse-context attributes for this sentence are The speaker treats the statement as already true or established — no hedging, no doubt, full confidence.
The particle appears anywhere other than the front — mid-sentence or at the end.
No clear orientation toward listener agreement; a plain statement or ambiguous stance.
The speaker expresses happiness, excitement, enthusiasm, satisfaction, humour, affection, or relief.
Phrased as a question but does not expect a genuine answer; used to make a point or emphasise something..

Particle meanings:
  ke  : signals genuine uncertainty or invites the listener to confirm something the speaker is unsure about.
  kan : signals assumed shared knowledge and seeks light confirmation of something the speaker already believes ("right?").
  neutral : indicates no particle.

Your task is to predict which particle, either “ke,” “kan,” or “neutral,” belongs in the [___] slot, such that the discourse-context attributes for this sentence are The speaker treats the statement as already true or established — no hedging, no doubt, full confidence.
The particle appears anywhere other than the front — mid-sentence or at the end.
The speaker treats the information as shared or obvious — the underlying tone is 'you already know this'.
No detectable emotional charge, or the emotion is genuinely ambiguous.
Phrased as a question but does not expect a genuine answer; used to make a point or emphasise something..

Particle meanings:
  ke  : signals genuine uncertainty or invites the listener to confirm something the speaker is unsure about.
  kan : signals assumed shared knowledge and seeks light confirmation of something the speaker already believes ("right?").
  neutral : indicates no particle.

Return exactly one word from this set and nothing else: ke, kan, neutral
constraint: You are a linguist specialising in colloquial Malay discourse particles. You must output exactly one word — either "ke" or "kan" or "neutral" — and nothing else.

Your task is to predict which particle, either “ke,” “kan,” or “neutral,” belongs in the [___] slot, such that the discourse-context attributes for this sentence are The speaker treats the statement as already true or established — no hedging, no doubt, full confidence.
The particle appears at the very start of the sentence, before any other content words.
The speaker treats the information as shared or obvious — the underlying tone is 'you already know this'.
The speaker expresses happiness, excitement, enthusiasm, satisfaction, humour, affection, or relief.
Phrased as a question but does not expect a genuine answer; used to make a point or emphasise something..

Particle meanings:
  ke  : signals genuine uncertainty or invites the listener to confirm something the speaker is unsure about.
  kan : signals assumed shared knowledge and seeks light confirmation of something the speaker already believes ("right?").
  neutral : indicates no particle.

Your task is to predict which particle, either “ke,” “kan,” or “neutral,” belongs in the [___] slot, such that the discourse-context attributes for this sentence are The speaker sounds unsure, is making a guess, estimating, or checking whether something is the case.
The particle appears anywhere other than the front — mid-sentence or at the end.
The speaker is actively checking whether the listener agrees or can confirm something.
No detectable emotional charge, or the emotion is genuinely ambiguous.
A genuine question inviting the listener to confirm or deny; the speaker does not already know the answer..

Particle meanings:
  ke  : signals genuine uncertainty or invites the listener to confirm something the speaker is unsure about.
  kan : signals assumed shared knowledge and seeks light confirmation of something the speaker already believes ("right?").
  neutral : indicates no particle.

Your task is to predict which particle, either “ke,” “kan,” or “neutral,” belongs in the [___] slot, such that the discourse-context attributes for this sentence are The speaker treats the statement as already true or established — no hedging, no doubt, full confidence.

No clear orientation toward listener agreement; a plain statement or ambiguous stance.
No detectable emotional charge, or the emotion is genuinely ambiguous.
The sentence makes an assertion or conveys information — not structured as a question..

Particle meanings:
  ke  : signals genuine uncertainty or invites the listener to confirm something the speaker is unsure about.
  kan : signals assumed shared knowledge and seeks light confirmation of something the speaker already believes ("right?").
  neutral : indicates no particle.

Your task is to predict which particle, either “ke,” “kan,” or “neutral,” belongs in the [___] slot, such that the discourse-context attributes for this sentence are The speaker treats the statement as already true or established — no hedging, no doubt, full confidence.

The speaker treats the information as shared or obvious — the underlying tone is 'you already know this'.
The speaker expresses happiness, excitement, enthusiasm, satisfaction, humour, affection, or relief.
The sentence makes an assertion or conveys information — not structured as a question..

Particle meanings:
  ke  : signals genuine uncertainty or invites the listener to confirm something the speaker is unsure about.
  kan : signals assumed shared knowledge and seeks light confirmation of something the speaker already believes ("right?").
  neutral : indicates no particle.

Speaker:
  "@simplyjoyaa betul betul betul, kita [___] harus baik"



Using the sentence context and the attributes above, which single particle — "ke" or "kan" or "neutral" — best fills [___]?

Return exactly one word from this set and nothing else: ke, kan, neutral
constraint: You are a linguist specialising in colloquial Malay discourse particles. You must output exactly one word — either "ke" or "kan" or "neutral" — and nothing else.

Your task is to predict which particle, either “ke,” “kan,” or “neutral,” belongs in the [___] slot, such that the discourse-context attributes for this sentence are The speaker sounds unsure, is making a guess, estimating, or checking whether something is the case.
The particle appears anywhere other than the front — mid-sentence or at the end.
The speaker is actively checking whether the listener agrees or can confirm something.
No detectable emotional charge, or the emotion is genuinely ambiguous.
A genuine question inviting the listener to confirm or deny; the speaker does not already know the answer..

Particle meanings:
  ke  : signals genuine uncertainty or invites the listener to confirm something the speaker is unsure about.
  kan : signals assumed shared knowledge and seeks light confirmation of something the speaker already believes ("right?").
  neutral : indicates no particle.

Speaker:
  "Yang buat ni, nak masuk melaka [___]?"

Return exactly one word from this set and nothing else: ke, kan, neutral
constraint: You are a linguist specialising in colloquial Malay discourse particles. You must output exactly one word — either "ke" or "kan" or "neutral" — and nothing else.

The sentence makes an assertion or conveys information — not structured as a question..

Particle meanings:

Your task is to predict which particle, either “ke,” “kan,” or “neutral,” belongs in the [___] slot, such that the discourse-context attributes for this sentence are The speaker sounds unsure, is making a guess, estimating, or checking whether something is the case.
The particle appears anywhere other than the front — mid-sentence or at the end.
The speaker is actively checking whether the listener agrees or can confirm something.
No detectable emotional charge, or the emotion is genuinely ambiguous.
A genuine question inviting the listener to confirm or deny; the speaker does not already know the answer..

Particle meanings:
  ke  : signals genuine uncertainty or invites the listener to confirm something the speaker is unsure about.
  kan : signals assumed shared knowledge and seeks light confirmation of something the speaker already believes ("right?").
  neutral : indicates no particle.

Speaker:
  "Orang Sabah&Sarawak : Soalan Babi Hutan i dengar cerita, hampir semua babi hutan dah lesap dari hutan sabah dan sarawak? 

betul [___]?"

Return exactly one word from this set and nothing else: ke, kan, neutral
constraint: You are a linguist specialising in colloquial Malay discourse particles. You must output exactly one word — either "ke" or "kan" or "neutral" — and nothing else.

Your task is to predict which particle, either “ke,” “kan,” or “neutral,” belongs in the [___] slot, such that the discourse-context attributes for this sentence are The speaker sounds unsure, is making a guess, estimating, or checking whether something is the case.

The speaker is actively checking whether the listener agrees or can confirm something.
No detectable emotional charge, or the emotion is genuinely ambiguous.
A genuine question inviting the listener to confirm or deny; the speaker does not already know the answer..

Particle meanings:
  ke  : signals genuine uncertainty or invites the listener to confirm something the speaker is unsure about.
  kan : signals assumed shared knowledge and seeks light confirmation of something the speaker already believes ("right?").
  neutral : indicates no particle.

Korang tau [___] resolusi?? Yang 1080p, 480p, 360p e.t.c.
Rasanya sama je lah macam video ajar tu.."

Your task is to predict which particle, either “ke,” “kan,” or “neutral,” belongs in the [___] slot, such that the discourse-context attributes for this sentence are The speaker sounds unsure, is making a guess, estimating, or checking whether something is the case.

The speaker is actively checking whether the listener agrees or can confirm something.
No detectable emotional charge, or the emotion is genuinely ambiguous.
A genuine question inviting the listener to confirm or deny; the speaker does not already know the answer..

Particle meanings:
  ke  : signals genuine uncertainty or invites the listener to confirm something the speaker is unsure about.
  kan : signals assumed shared knowledge and seeks light confirmation of something the speaker already believes ("right?").
  neutral : indicates no particle.

Speaker:
  "@Pelabur_Bijak Newbie here. Kalau beli dekat Miga, lepastu jual saja dekat Miga. Takde issue [___]? Tak perlu jadikan fizikal. Betul tak?"



Using the sentence context and the attributes above, which single particle — "ke" or "kan" or "neutral" — best fills [___]?

Return exactly one word from this set and nothing else: ke, kan, neutral
constraint: You are a linguist specialising in colloquial Malay discourse particles. You must output exactly one word — either "ke" or "kan" or "neutral" — and nothing else.
